---
title: "Manufacturing Process Analytics"
subtitle: "Stage 1 of 3 — Data-Driven Engineering Portfolio"
author: "Shikhar Sinha | Mechanical Engineering"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Contents"
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

## Executive Summary

> This project analyzes 10,000 machine operation cycles from the AI4I 2020
> Predictive Maintenance dataset to identify the root causes of equipment failure.
> Using statistical analysis and feature engineering, **Rotational Speed (RPM)**, 
> **Tool Wear**, and **Mechanical Power** were identified as the dominant failure drivers,
> accounting for **80%** of all failures. Failure rate escalates from **2.09%**
> in fresh tools to **15.5%** in the critical wear zone.

## Problem Statement

Manufacturing downtime costs industrial facilities an estimated $50 billion
annually in the US alone. Identifying which process parameters precede
equipment failure is the first step toward predictive maintenance strategies
that reduce unplanned downtime.

**Research question:** Which measurable process variables most strongly
precede machine failure, and can we quantify a threshold beyond which
failure risk escalates significantly?

## Dataset

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Source   | UCI ML Repository — AI4I 2020 (ID: 601) |
| Rows     | 10,000 machine cycles |
| Features | 14 (5 sensor + 1 categorical + 5 failure modes + 1 ID) |
| Failure rate | ~3.39% |
| Failure modes | TWF, HDF, PWF, OSF, RNF |

## Key Findings

1. **Overstrain** was elevated by **70.6%** in failed runs vs. normal runs (p < 0.001)
2. Failure rate escalates from **2.09%** (High Grade/Fresh wear) to **15.5%** (Critical wear)  
3. Low-quality (L) grade products fail at **1.88×** the rate of High-quality (H) products (3.92% vs 2.09%)

## Recommendations

1. Implement a tool wear alert threshold at **204.5** minutes to prevent critical overstrain triggers.
2. Alarm on rotational speeds dropping below a threshold of **1386.5 RPM** under heavy operation.
3. Prioritize preventive maintenance for L-grade product runs, given their higher statistical baseline risk.
